title: Local vs Remote Servers
concept: The tradeoffs of running a server on the user's machine versus a shared remote deployment.

exercises.prompt: In our last lesson, which transport combination did we use to connect to a remote server over the web?
exercises.explain: In the previous lesson, we learned that HTTP with Server-Sent Events (SSE) is the standard transport mechanism for connecting to remote MCP servers over the internet.

cards.title: Where Should Your Server Live?
cards.body: We know how to connect LLMs to servers using files (stdio) or the web (HTTP/SSE). But how do you decide where your MCP server should actually live? Choosing between your own machine and the cloud changes everything about speed, security, and setup.

cards.title: Local vs. Remote MCP
cards.body: A local MCP server runs directly on your computer, communicating instantly through standard input/output (stdio). A remote MCP server runs on a cloud platform or external machine, requiring web protocols like HTTP and Server-Sent Events (SSE) to talk to your LLM.

cards.title: The Kitchen Analogy
cards.body: Think of a local server like a private chef cooking right in your kitchen—it is fast and has direct access to your fridge, but only you can eat there. A remote server is like a restaurant kitchen—anyone can order from it over the delivery network, but it takes longer to get your food.

cards.title: Real-World Setups
cards.body: If you want an LLM to read your local codebase files, you run a local MCP server. If your team wants a shared LLM tool that queries a live weather API or a cloud database, you host it as a remote MCP server.

cards.title: Is Cloud Always Better?
cards.body: You might think remote cloud servers are always better because they are 'in the cloud.' Actually, local servers are often much safer for private code because your files never leave your machine to interact with the tool.

cards.title: Architectural Face-Off
cards.body: Choosing your transport architecture depends on where your data lives and who needs access to it.

left.text: Uses stdio, super fast, accesses local files, offline-ready.

right.text: Uses HTTP/SSE, shared with others, hosts remote APIs, requires internet.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a primary benefit of using a remote MCP server?

options.text: It has direct access to your laptop's local file system without internet.
It is completely immune to network disconnects.
It can be shared easily among multiple developers and users.
It communicates strictly using stdio pipes.

exercises.explain: Remote servers run on external infrastructure, which makes them perfect for sharing API keys and databases across a whole team of developers.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A local MCP server requires an active internet connection to communicate with a local LLM client.
exercises.explain: Local servers communicate via stdio (standard input/output), which works entirely offline on your physical machine.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe the speed advantage of local servers:
exercises.sentence: Because local servers do not send data over the internet, they suffer from much less latency than remote servers.
exercises.explain: Local servers communicate directly through computer memory and pipes (stdio), meaning they have almost zero network latency compared to sending requests over the internet.

exercises.prompt: Identify the AI-generated thought on local vs remote MCP transport:

options.text: I like local servers because I don't have to configure domain names or SSL certificates just to test a prompt.
The deployment of remote MCP instances facilitates centralized state management but introduces transport layer overhead via HTTP/SSE protocols.
If my internet goes down, my local MCP tools still work fine, which is a lifesaver when traveling.

exercises.explain: Option B is clearly written by an AI trying to sound like a textbook, using stiff phrasing and repeating 'MCP' and 'transport protocols' unnecessarily. Options A and C represent natural conversational human thoughts.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are designing a coding assistant tool that needs to edit files on your laptop and also search a shared company database. How should you split these MCP servers?

options.text: Local servers require SSE streaming, while remote servers only use standard standard input/output.
Local servers run on your machine with direct file access, while remote servers run externally and require secure network protocols.
Remote servers are always faster because they bypass the internet entirely.
Local servers cannot be accessed by your local LLM client at all.

exercises.explain: Local MCP servers have direct access to your local files and local command line, making them highly secure and fast for personal automation. Remote MCP servers, however, require secure network protocols (like HTTPS/SSE) because they run on external hardware and are accessed over the internet, making them great for shared team tools but harder to connect to local files.
